title: Account Management Guide
content:
In NimbusDesk, account management is controlled from Settings > Account. Every workspace has one owner, optional admins, and standard members. The owner can transfer ownership, deactivate users, and manage billing permissions. Admins can invite users, assign roles, and control most operational settings, but they cannot delete the workspace or change legal invoicing data unless owner permission is granted.

To invite a new member, open Team, click Invite User, enter their email, and choose a role. Invitations expire in 7 days. If a user does not receive an invite, ask them to check spam or allowlist notifications@nimbusdesk.app. You can resend invites from Team > Pending Invitations.

For role changes, select a user row and update role access. Role changes are applied immediately and logged in the activity audit trail. If your security policy requires approval, enable Role Change Approval under Security Settings.

To deactivate a user, open Team, click the user, and choose Deactivate. Deactivated users lose access instantly, but their historical comments and ticket actions remain for compliance. You can reactivate them later.

Workspace profile settings include company name, logo, timezone, default language, and business hours. These defaults affect reports, reminder schedules, and SLA calculations. If business hours are changed, SLA counters recalculate for future tickets only.

For SSO-enabled organizations, account lifecycle should be managed from your identity provider. NimbusDesk will sync user status during login and scheduled sync intervals.

Best practice: review account roles monthly, keep at least two admins for continuity, and enable audit logs for regulated environments.

title: Billing and Invoices
content:
NimbusDesk billing is subscription-based and charged per workspace. Billing settings are available to owners and finance-enabled admins from Settings > Billing. The platform supports monthly and annual billing cycles. Annual plans receive a discounted rate compared to monthly pricing.

Invoices are generated automatically at the start of each billing period and can be downloaded as PDF from Billing > Invoices. Invoice fields include legal entity name, tax ID, billing address, plan tier, seat count, add-ons, and tax details. If your accounting team needs purchase order references, add a PO number under Billing Profile before invoice generation.

Payment methods supported include major credit cards and selected regional bank debit options. You can add multiple payment methods and set one as default. If a payment fails, NimbusDesk retries according to a scheduled dunning sequence and sends alerts to billing contacts.

Seat-based charges are prorated when users are added or removed mid-cycle. Upgrading plans applies immediately, while downgrades usually take effect at the next renewal date unless explicitly confirmed with immediate feature reduction.

To change your billing cycle, go to Billing > Plan and choose monthly or annual. Some enterprise contracts are fixed-term and cannot be switched self-service.

For tax compliance, ensure billing profile data remains accurate. VAT/GST treatment depends on your jurisdiction and tax status. If your tax exemption should apply, upload exemption certificates via Billing > Tax Documents.

If an account becomes overdue beyond the grace period, workspace access may be restricted to read-only until payment is resolved.

title: Password Reset and Login Recovery
content:
If you cannot sign in to NimbusDesk, use the password recovery flow from the login page by selecting Forgot Password. Enter your account email and submit the form. If an account exists, a reset link is sent by email. For security reasons, the interface does not confirm whether the email is registered.

Reset links are time-limited and single-use. Open the link, choose a strong new password, and confirm. Password requirements include minimum length, mixed character classes, and exclusion of common leaked password patterns. After a successful reset, active sessions may be invalidated depending on your workspace security policy.

If the reset email does not arrive within a few minutes, check spam folders, corporate email filtering, and allowlist noreply@nimbusdesk.app. You can request a new link if the previous one has expired. Only the latest reset request remains valid.

If your workspace uses Single Sign-On, local password reset may be disabled. In that case, reset credentials through your identity provider such as Okta, Azure AD, or Google Workspace. NimbusDesk cannot override IdP-controlled authentication.

When two-factor authentication is enabled and you lose your authenticator device, use backup codes during login recovery. If backup codes are unavailable, contact a workspace admin. Admins can trigger identity verification and issue a temporary recovery window.

For locked accounts caused by repeated failed login attempts, a short cooldown period is applied automatically. Security alerts are sent to the account email to help detect suspicious access attempts.

Best practice: enable MFA, store backup codes securely, and avoid password reuse across services.

title: Notification Preferences and Alerts
content:
NimbusDesk sends notifications for ticket updates, mentions, assignment changes, SLA risks, and account events. Notification preferences can be managed per user in Profile > Notifications and at workspace level in Settings > Workspace Notifications.

Users can configure channel-specific preferences for email, in-app, and webhook-based alerts where available. For email digests, choose immediate delivery or batched schedules such as hourly and daily summaries. Critical incident alerts can be forced as immediate regardless of digest settings.

Workspace admins can define default notification rules for new members. These defaults include assignment alerts, mention alerts, and SLA warning thresholds. Individual users may override non-mandatory preferences.

Quiet hours can be enabled to suppress non-critical notifications outside configured times. Quiet-hour behavior follows each user timezone unless workspace policy enforces a central timezone. Emergency alerts can bypass quiet hours if Incident Priority Override is enabled.

For integrations, notification routing supports channels like Slack and Microsoft Teams through official connectors. Routing rules can filter by project, severity, or tag. If notifications are not received in integrated channels, verify connector authorization and channel permissions.

Email delivery troubleshooting steps include confirming recipient domain policies, checking SPF/DKIM alignment, and reviewing suppressed-address logs. Repeated bounces may automatically place an address on temporary suppression to protect sender reputation.

Audit logs capture notification policy changes, including who changed rules and when. This is useful for incident review and compliance reporting.

title: API Usage and Access Tokens
content:
NimbusDesk provides a REST API for ticket operations, user management, reporting, and automation workflows. API documentation is available in the Developer Portal. To call the API, create a personal or service token in Settings > Developer > API Tokens.

Each token has scopes controlling what it can access, such as tickets:read, tickets:write, users:read, and reports:read. Use least-privilege scope assignment to reduce risk. Tokens should never be embedded in frontend code or shared in plain text over chat.

Requests must include the Authorization header with Bearer token. API responses are JSON and include pagination metadata for list endpoints. Standard HTTP status codes are used: 200 for success, 201 for created resources, 400 for validation errors, 401 for unauthorized, 403 for forbidden, 404 for not found, and 429 for rate-limited requests.

Rate limits are applied per token and endpoint class. When limits are exceeded, the API returns 429 with retry hints. Implement exponential backoff and idempotency keys for safe retries on write operations.

Webhook subscriptions can be configured to receive event callbacks for ticket created, ticket updated, comment added, and SLA breached. Verify webhook signatures to ensure authenticity.

For production integrations, rotate tokens regularly, monitor token usage logs, and revoke unused credentials. If a token is compromised, revoke it immediately and create a replacement with narrowed scopes.

API versioning uses a stable path strategy. Breaking changes are introduced only in new versions with migration guidance and deprecation notice timelines.

title: Subscription Plans and Upgrades
content:
NimbusDesk offers Starter, Growth, and Enterprise subscription plans. Each plan includes core ticketing and collaboration, but feature limits vary by tier. Starter is intended for small teams with basic workflows, Growth adds advanced automation and analytics, and Enterprise includes SSO, advanced security controls, and premium support options.

Plan details and current usage are available in Settings > Billing > Plan. Usage metrics include active seats, automation runs, storage consumption, and API request volume. If usage exceeds plan limits, certain actions may be throttled or restricted until limits reset or the plan is upgraded.

To upgrade, choose a higher plan and confirm billing changes. Upgrades generally activate immediately and prorated charges appear on the next invoice or instant adjustment invoice depending on billing settings. Downgrades are scheduled for renewal by default to avoid abrupt feature loss.

Feature gating is explicit: if a workspace downgrades below required capabilities, related configurations remain visible in read-only mode until removed or the plan is increased again. For example, SSO settings may be disabled on non-Enterprise tiers.

Enterprise agreements can include custom SLAs, dedicated onboarding, and negotiated data retention terms. These are reflected in contract metadata rather than standard self-service controls.

Before changing plans, review impacted automations, storage allowances, and user role mappings. It is recommended to export critical reports and notify admins before scheduled downgrades.

title: Data Export and Retention
content:
NimbusDesk supports data export for compliance, migration, and backup workflows. Export options are available in Settings > Data Management for authorized admins. Exportable data categories include tickets, comments, users, attachments metadata, audit logs, and selected analytics summaries.

You can request a full workspace export or filtered exports by date range, project, or status. Large exports are processed asynchronously and made available as downloadable archives with expiration links. Notification emails are sent when export jobs complete.

Attachment binaries may be included depending on your plan and export settings. For very large data volumes, staged export packages are generated to improve reliability. Checksums are provided for integrity verification.

Retention policies define how long records are kept. Default retention can be overridden by legal hold rules for specific projects or user groups. Deleting a ticket from the UI does not always imply immediate physical deletion; records may remain in retention state for a policy-defined period.

If your organization must satisfy regulatory requirements, configure retention rules before large-scale usage. Audit logs track export requests, including requester, scope, timestamps, and completion status.

To perform recurring backups, use the API export endpoints and store encrypted archives in your secure storage. Validate restore procedures periodically so backups are operationally useful.

When an account is terminated, data handling follows contractual and legal obligations. If required, request a final export before closure and review deletion confirmation timelines.

title: Security Settings and Compliance Controls
content:
NimbusDesk security settings are managed in Settings > Security. Key controls include multi-factor authentication enforcement, single sign-on, session timeout policies, IP allowlists, audit logging, and role-based access control.

MFA can be optional or mandatory. When enforced, users must complete second-factor enrollment at next login. Supported factors typically include authenticator apps and backup codes. Admins should communicate enrollment deadlines before enabling mandatory enforcement.

Session security options allow administrators to define idle timeouts, absolute session lifetime, and concurrent session limits. For high-security environments, reduce timeout windows and require re-authentication for sensitive actions.

IP allowlisting can restrict access to approved corporate networks. If enabled incorrectly, administrators may lock out legitimate users, so test policies with staged rollout and fallback admin access.

SSO integration centralizes identity and enables automated deprovisioning. Map IdP groups to NimbusDesk roles to reduce manual permission drift. Periodically review role mapping outcomes and failed login logs.

Audit logs provide immutable records for authentication events, permission changes, data exports, and billing updates. Compliance teams can filter logs and export reports for SOC 2 and internal reviews.

Security alerts notify admins of unusual sign-in patterns, repeated failed logins, and suspicious token usage. Respond by rotating credentials, revoking sessions, and reviewing recent audit events.

Best practice checklist: enforce MFA, enable SSO for managed users, restrict admin roles, review audit logs weekly, and maintain an incident response runbook.
